chunks.text: Ladies and gentlemen, a mom emailed me and she says she has an atheist son who says that God must be evil because he created creatures who do horrible things. How would I respond? Michael from Ireland writes in and asks, how can it be that the Bible says that God appoints leaders, but it also says Satan is the god of this world? Doesn't that seem a bit contradictory? Also, a woman writes in who's planning her wedding, but she wants to get the gospel to unbelievers at the wedding. How can she do that? She's unsure about how to do it, so I'll give my advice. But before we get to all those questions, these apologetic questions, I wanna talk a little bit about Independence Day. It is coming up. You know, we just had a tour by the Speaker of the House a couple of podcasts ago. You can watch it on our YouTube channel. One of the guests, in addition to me there, was my friend Steve Dace, who does a daily program with Blaze Radio, and of course, it's on YouTube as well. And he's talking about Independence Day for kids. And I don't know about you, ladies and gentlemen, I'm just gonna be honest. Sometimes if I wanna learn something and get the essence of it, I read a kid's book because the author is forced to tell the truth in a very simple way. I'm just gonna be honest, sometimes I do that, and I learn from kids' books. And Steve has a great new kids' book out called Why Independence Day: America is great because God is good. So here he is, ladies and gentlemen, the great Steve Dace. He's gonna tell us a little bit about that. He's coming all the way... Okay, enough of the crowd. You're crowd is noisy, Steve. All the way from Des Moines, Iowa, where he does his program. Steve, for those people out there who may not know who you are, you're doing a daily program, it's one of the top programs out there, particularly spiritual religious programs. How did you get to where you are? What's your background? First of all, good to see you as always, Frank. I started in the mailroom. My wife has always threatened that she's gonna write an autobiography. It's gonna be titled, "I Married a Mailroom Clerk." You know? And so I was voted most likely to succeed in high school and teacher's headache in the same yearbook. I was that kid growing up, you know? But I got by on a lot of just kind of God-given ability and not a great work ethic, and kind of crashed and burned when I went out there on my own. And I moved back to the state where I was born in Iowa. I grew up in Michigan, but I moved back here and lived in my grandma's house rent-free trying to start all over again, and I worked in the mailroom at Blue Cross Blue Shield. And through there, I talked my way into a part-time news assistant job at the Des Moines Register in the sports department. After a year or two there, they finally gave me a chance to start covering minor league hockey because I was from Michigan and I was the only person in the entire sports department that knew anything at all about hockey. And just from there, I kind of worked my way up. I started doing local sports talk radio as a guest host. They gave me a sports talk radio show at that point, and then that became so successful here in town that the big Clear Channel blowtorch WHO here in Des Moines where Ronald Reagan was our first sports director, came to me and said, "Hey, do you want to be our afternoon news talk guy?" And I said, "Sure." You know? And I, by this time now, I've had a spiritual conversion at age 30. And so I still love sports, but I kind of feel like maybe just this prodigious memory I have and this stuff I've just always been able to kind of naturally do and say, maybe there's a purpose behind it more than just breaking down box scores. Right? So I was getting more interested in... And having more of a voice in broader issues anyway. I've always been interested in politics. I was in Young Republicans, and all that stuff growing up. Alex P. Keaton was like a role model of mine when I was a kid, you know? And so we make the switch over there. And I have no idea the amount of influence that this show is gonna provide me. I thought I legit thought I was just doing a talk show until the morning after the 2008 Iowa caucuses. And I essentially was a Mike Huckabee super PAC during that cycle. And he pulled a big upset. And the next morning, my general manager called me into his office, and on his desk, he had the WHO coverage map that he always had there. And he took the front page of the Des Moines Register, which had the county-by-county map results of who won every one of our 99 counties, Romney or Huckabee. And he pulled it out and he laid it over our coverage map, and with the exception of one county, Frank, it was an exact replica. I mean, it was a complete and total symbiosis. And he looked at me and he said, "Congratulations on your big win last night." And that's when I began to see that there was way more possibility for this than just, you know, selling live reads and widgets and entertaining people. And so I turned my show into a vehicle for activism. And they thought, well, maybe they could do that with somebody like me. And so they gave me the money to go out and to try to build my own thing. And it took a long time, a lot of stops and starts. It's very hard, Frank, to do this in the for-profit world without selling out to somebody, frankly. It's really hard, okay? So, you know, the quote, the great prophets Sawyer Brown, the country band from when I was a kid, we had to take the dirt road to get here. I mean, it took a long winding road, Paul McCartney, to get this far. But eventually, we worked our way up steadily. One of the last acts that Stu Sr. did as chairman of the board was recommend that they invent a time slot for me, so they created one, 9 p.m. to midnight, put me on about 100 stations around the country to see how I would do. And the show grew quite a bit. And eventually, we went digital with a company called Conservative Review, and then they merged. And that was Mark Levin. Hey, back in the day, and we've been good friends ever since. And I'll just share with you, Frank, what I texted to Hogan right after I got back from that event. I could not have been more impressed in that setting, Frank. Yeah, no, he was very impressive. And when he said he was an attorney for ADF for at least a decade, I immediately got it. I get, oh, Alliance Defending Freedom. He was one of their attorneys. That's why he's so articulate. That's why he knows the history of the country. That's why I can be confident he's an evangelical Christian because he worked for ADF, which is an organization we've been supporting forever. And we're already trying to arrange another tour with him because I want more people to see who he is and also see what goes on in the Capitol and see the history of the Capitol from the proper perspective. And Satan himself has to get these good qualities from God in order to exist or do anything himself. Now, you might say, well, okay, but why does God allow these horrible things to occur? That's something I'm going to address in the next question, which is related to this. And this question comes from Michael from Ireland, who writes in and asks, how can it be that the Bible says that God appoints leaders, say leaders of nations, but it also says that Satan is the god of this world? And for example, some of the passages where the biblical writers and even Jesus himself talk about Satan being the god of this world. Paul in 2 Corinthians 4.4 says, he refers to Satan as the god of this world. Jesus refers to Satan as the ruler, or some translations might say the prince of this world. Paul in Ephesians 2 says of Satan, the prince of the power of the air. And John writes in 1 John 5.19 that the whole world is in the power of the evil one. Now, what do these verses mean? Does it mean that Satan has all the power and God has no power? No, it doesn't mean that. These verses are usually understood to mean that Satan has influence over the fallen world order, not that he owns the earth absolutely or is equal to God in power. That would be impossible. So in other words, the Bible presents Satan as a real spiritual enemy with broad influence, but that influence is still under God's ultimate authority. God still gives Satan some power, but not ultimate power. He has him on a leash, sort of like us, right? He gives us power to make choices. He could stop us if he wanted to, but he doesn't choose to stop us because if he does stop us, then we're not moral creatures and this isn't a morally relevant world. What he does instead is he ultimately quarantines us in a place called hell. And that is where we ultimately wind up. So Michael, when the Bible says that God appoints leaders, and he does, but Satan is the power of this world, Satan can influence, obviously, but he doesn't have the same power as God. He still is subordinate to the power of God. God allows him freedom like he allows us freedom. Now, why would he allow his freedom? Well, he allows Satan freedom and us freedom and demons freedom and angels freedom because without freedom, this couldn't be a world of love. We wouldn't have the opportunity to love. The problem is when we have that opportunity to love, we can use that free will to do evil. You say, okay, well, why doesn't God just stop it? Well, as I mentioned a minute ago, he might influence certain events to not occur, but a lot of times he lets them occur and he could redeem those events. I mean, if you think about the fact that God has to give us free will to allow love, which allows us to make moral or immoral choices, God can bring good from those evil choices, good that sometimes we can see in good times and good sometimes that we can't see. One of the things we've talked about, and I talk about this a lot in the book Stealing from God, is the idea that you can learn good things from pain and suffering. It's called like a soul building theodicy, explaining why God allows certain evils. For example, it's hard to learn courage unless there's danger. It's hard to learn compassion unless somebody is suffering.

It's hard to really think about others if you get everything you want all the time. You know, what do we call children who get everything they want? Spoiled. What's spoiled about them? Their character. You give a kid everything he wants, you're gonna ruin that kid. A kid needs people to say no. He needs people to put boundaries on him. He needs difficulty, like we all need difficulty in our lives, if we're gonna become more like Jesus rather than more like Satan. If you keep getting everything you want all the time, you're gonna become a complete me monster. I know I would. I'd be more of a me monster than I already am. There has to be difficulty. There has to be pushback. There have to be problems in this life in order to mold you into what Jesus wants to mold you into. In fact, the famous passage in Romans 8, people stop reading, but it starts this way. It says, we know that all things work together for good to those that love God and are called according to His purpose. Now, it doesn't say all things are good, work together for good. But then it goes on to say, in order to be conformed to the image of His Son, God allows certain evils so we can be conformed to the image of His Son. That's one reason that God allows certain evils. In fact, Jesus Himself, it says in the book of Hebrews, it says He learned suffering through obedience. Or let me put it another way, He learned obedience through suffering. Let me get it right. He learned obedience through suffering. If Jesus, who didn't have a sin nature, had to learn obedience through suffering, what about us that do have a sin nature? Yes, and God can redeem evil. God can bring good from evil, even when we can't see it. This is known as, you know, the ripple effect. We've talked about this on several occasions here on this program. Why does a certain evil occur? Sometimes we don't know. In fact, I was meeting with a friend of mine who lost his 20-year-old daughter to an illness that came out of nowhere. And this is a couple of years after this happened, so he's asking questions. I wouldn't say this the day it happened, obviously, but we got talking about it. And he's seeking answers. And I said, well, when I see something like this happen, I don't know why it happened, but I know why I don't know why it happened. Because I'm finite. I don't know the end from the beginning. But a being outside of this universe who knows eternity is coming and can see how all these interacting choices ripple forward to affect so many other choices and so many other people and so many other events, I can see that I might not, not might not, I don't have adequate information to say there's no reason that, say, his daughter died or a baby died or some terrible event like Charlie being murdered, that there's no reason downstream that this could bring great good. Steve just talked about the fact that that memorial service for Charlie was the greatest evangelical event in history in terms of people hearing the gospel. It was. Over a billion people have seen some of that memorial service. That's a great good that came from a great evil. We can't see how all the ripples will wind up, but God can. That's why we can trust God even when we can't see and it seems inexplicable to us. Why did my 20-year-old daughter die? Why did a baby die? Why did this evil thing happen? I don't know why. Well, I know why generally it happened because we live in a fallen world. I mean, if everyone was indestructible until age 70 or 80, we'd be completely reckless until then, especially in our fallen state. Now when we get to heaven, we won't have the fallen state. We won't have the sin nature, so that issue does not apply, but here it does. But I know why in general bad things happen because we live in a fallen world. But if you're going to ask me why did this bad thing happen, I don't know why, but I know why I don't know why. I'm inside of time. I know very little of the past, very little of the present, nothing of the future except what God tells me. How do I know where this is going? I don't. I have to trust God. And the other point that needs to be made with regard to evil is that Christianity is the answer to the problem of evil. If we had never sinned, Jesus would have no reason to add humanity to His deity and come to earth and allow the creatures that rebelled against Him to torture and kill Him so He could then take our punishment upon Himself. And then by trusting in Him, we're not only forgiven, but given His righteousness. He would have no reason to do that if we had never sinned. So God entered the bloodstream of humanity in order to be the perfect man so the infinite justice of the Father came down on Him rather than us. That way, God remains just and is the justifier of the one who has faith in Jesus, as Paul explains in Romans 3:26. So Christianity is the answer to the problem of evil. Now, I would ask your son, ma'am, and Michael also wrote in, if someone is not a Christian, I think you should ask them the question, if Christianity were true, would you become a Christian? And see what they say. Or if you want to leave Christianity out of it because it might have a negative connotation in somebody's mind, you might say, if Jesus really predicted and accomplished His own resurrection from the dead to prove He was God, would you follow Him? And see what they say. If they hesitate or say no, it's not a head problem, it's a heart problem. You can move on from there. All right, let me go to the final question. A woman writes in a long email. I won't read the whole thing, but she basically is a shy person, but she wants her friends and relatives who don't know Jesus to know Jesus through her wedding. So what kind of advice could I give her? She said, what should I ask the officiant to say? And I'll say this, I think a wedding is a beautiful opportunity to demonstrate what's really going on in heaven and why we're really here. And so if the officiant could say something like, do you know that the Bible starts and ends with a wedding? It does. It starts with Adam and Eve and it ends with the great wedding. And everybody's invited to the wedding. And who is the bride? We are, if we choose to be. And who is the bridegroom? Jesus. In fact, if you want to sum up the entire purpose of why we're here and sum up what the Bible, the central message is, it's five words. Jesus wants to marry you. But He's not going to force you into a marriage against your will. He wants to marry you and the marriage that we see here on earth is an illustration of our marriage to Jesus. In fact, the whole wedding ceremony is patterned after the ancient practice of a covenant. If you go back to the covenant that Abraham had with God, I don't know if you remember this, probably it's Genesis 12 or 15. I'm trying to remember exactly where this happened. But there is a aisle and there are animals that are slain on each side of the aisle. And the covenant partners would pass through the center, this aisle of the slain animals, saying that if they failed to keep up their end of the covenant, they would become like the slain animals. Now in Abraham's case, Abraham is asleep when God, as depicted by a flaming torch, goes through the aisle of the two animals being slain, the aisle between them. He is demonstrating this is a one-way covenant. Abraham, it doesn't matter what you do, I'm going to keep the covenant. But this is where we get the idea that there's an aisle in the center and that the people who are witnessing a wedding are there not just to witness the wedding, but to help the two people in the marriage before God keep the covenant. This is why, of course, you can't go to a same-sex wedding because you can't affirm as a Christian, You want to keep them in that relationship that will keep them out of the kingdom. It's a sinful relationship, but I digress. The point here is that the two people standing there at the altar are in a covenant with one another before God and the people in the pews are there to not only witness, but help that couple in the future keep the covenant. Not to encourage them to get divorced when things go badly, but to encourage them to keep the covenant. And if you read the Scriptures, particularly Ephesians 5 on this, what is the bridegroom, the groom, what is he supposed to do for his wife? He is supposed to love the wife to the point that he will sacrifice himself to save her. What does that make you think of? Jesus. He's the Jesus figure. The groom in a wedding is the Jesus figure.

He's supposed to sacrifice himself in order to save the life of his wife. And what is the wife supposed to do? Respect her husband. And what are we supposed to do? And, of course, she's to love her husband as well. But what are we supposed to do in our relationship with God? God has sacrificed himself for us. We're to respect him and love him. And so the wedding is a beautiful illustration of what Christ did for us. That he sacrificed himself to save us. And we freely then love him back and respect him back. And that really is what a wedding is illustrating. It's illustrating our ultimate union to Jesus. And everyone is invited to this. And, of course, it's also important to point out, if you can, that love is not about feelings. This is not a feeling-based relationship. This is a covenant relationship. This is a love relationship. You can't feel or you can't vow to feel a certain way for the next 50 or 60 years. All you can avow is your behavior. That's why you have vows. If the relationship was completely based on feelings, you wouldn't have a vow. You'd say, as long as I feel this way, I'll stay in it. No, it's not about feelings. It's about a commitment to seek what's best for the other person according to God's will, regardless of what happens, for better or worse. And that's what Jesus does for us. Even when we're unfaithful, he's faithful. But we are commanded to love and we are vowing to love. You don't need that when you're lovey-dovey. You need that 10 years into the relationship when you wake up in the morning and you look over at your spouse when you wake up and you go, you again. Why did it have to be you again? Right? I mean, we're all impossible to live with sometimes, right? That's when you need the vow. And this is why God views divorce as such a great tragedy because God won't divorce us and he doesn't want us to divorce him. He doesn't want us to divorce one another. So the wedding is an illustration of the gospel. And oh, by the way, notice that traditionally, when a husband and wife get married, what does the wife do in terms of her name? She takes the name of her husband. What do we do when we become Christians? We take the name of our bridegroom, Jesus. We're Christians now. So you can illustrate all this right through the ceremony and point out these truths that what they are witnessing is a covenant before God and everyone is invited to marry God himself. And the bridegroom is Jesus. And it's up to you whether or not you want to accept his invitation. He's not gonna force you into a marriage against your will. But if you don't want his love and his sacrifice, then you're gonna have to pay for your own sins and you're gonna be judged by an infinitely just God. As we said before, there's only two possibilities in the end. You're either gonna get justice from an infinitely just God or you're gonna get grace by the God who sacrificed himself to take your punishment upon himself. Those are the two possibilities. It's up to you. So ma'am, I would encourage the officiant to talk about some of these things and make the explicit mention of the gospel so beautifully illustrated by the wedding ceremony. Make an explicit pitch, an explicit call for the repentance of people. If you don't know Jesus, here's the time to know him and then to follow him and be with him forever. So it's a beautiful opportunity. And thank you for wanting your loved ones who don't know the Lord to know him through the ceremony. It's a beautiful opportunity. All right, friends, if you wanna send a question, send it to hello at cross-examined.org. Hello at cross-examined.org. I'm sorry we can't get to all the questions, but we try and get to as many as we can. And don't forget that this weekend, we're gonna have Eric Metaxas on to talk about his amazing book, Revolution, which you're gonna wanna be a part of. You're gonna wanna get that book. It's so good. I've already read some of it and it's a wonderful, wonderful recounting of the spiritual heritage of our country that you should know and your kids should know. All right, great being with you. We'll see you here next time, Lord willing. God bless.

title: Be Worthy of the Bullet That Charlie Kirk Took For You — Why Independence Day? with Steve Deace PLUS Q&A
